title: Gulab Jamun

ingredients.title: For the Gulab Jamun
items: 1 cup khoya (mawa) – full-fat, fresh (essential for authentic texture)
1/4 tsp baking soda – optional, for fluffiness
1-2 tbsp milk – adjust to form soft dough
1 tsp rose water – optional, for extra aroma
Ghee or oil – for deep frying

ingredients.title: For the Sugar Syrup
items: 1 tsp rose water or saffron strands – optional, for flavor

instruction.title: Prepare the Sugar Syrup
steps: In a pan, combine sugar and water. Heat until sugar dissolves completely.
Add crushed cardamom and a few saffron strands.
Let it simmer for 5–7 minutes on low heat until slightly sticky.
Remove from heat and add rose water. Keep warm (don’t boil after adding rose water).

instruction.title: Make the Dough
steps: Crumble khoya into a bowl.
Add all-purpose flour and baking soda. Mix gently.
Gradually add milk, a teaspoon at a time, to form a soft, smooth, slightly sticky dough.
Tip: Over-kneading makes the dough tough; handle gently.
Let the dough rest for 10 minutes.

instruction.title: Shape the Gulab Jamun
steps: Divide the dough into small portions (about the size of a walnut).
Roll into smooth, crack-free balls.
Pro Tip: Smoothness is key to uniform texture and preventing cracks during frying.

instruction.title: Fry the Gulab Jamun
steps: Heat ghee or oil in a deep pan over low-medium heat.
Test with a small piece of dough: it should rise slowly and not brown too fast.
Fry the balls gently, stirring occasionally for even cooking.
Fry until golden brown on all sides (slow frying ensures a soft interior).

instruction.title: Soak in Syrup
steps: Remove fried gulab jamuns and immediately transfer into warm sugar syrup.
Let them soak for at least 1–2 hours (longer for richer sweetness).

instruction.title: Serving
steps: Serve warm or at room temperature.
Garnish with slivered pistachios or almonds.
Optional: drizzle a few drops of rose water before serving for extra aroma.

chefTips: Khoya quality: Fresh, full-fat khoya gives soft and melt-in-the-mouth gulab jamuns.
Frying: Low, slow frying is crucial. High heat will brown the exterior but leave the center raw.
Syrup consistency: Should be slightly sticky, not watery, to coat the gulab jamun perfectly.
